title: Minimum Characters to Add to Make Palindrome
description: Add characters either at front only (or both ends if you specify) to make palindrome, find minimum. Example: abcd (front-only) -> 3 (dcbabcd)

examples.explanation:
String: 'abcd', n=4
Check i=3: suffix 'd' is palindrome? Compare d with d → yes. But we need longest suffix, continue checking.
Check i=2: suffix 'cd' is palindrome? Compare c with d → no.
Check i=1: suffix 'bcd' is palindrome? Compare b with d → no.
Check i=0: suffix 'abcd' is palindrome? Compare a with d → no.
Longest palindrome suffix found at i=3: 'd'
Characters needed = i = 3
Add reverse of 'abc' = 'cba' to front: 'cba' + 'abcd' = 'cbabcd' (not palindrome yet)
Actually need 'dcb' + 'abcd' = 'dcbabcd' which is palindrome.
Minimum characters = 3

String: 'aaba', n=4
Check i=3: suffix 'a' is palindrome? Compare a with a → yes.
Check i=2: suffix 'ba' is palindrome? Compare b with a → no.
Check i=1: suffix 'aba' is palindrome? Compare a with a → yes, then b with b → yes. Full palindrome!
Found palindrome suffix at i=1: 'aba'
Characters needed = i = 1
Add 'a' to end: 'aaba' + 'a' = 'aabaa' which is palindrome.
Minimum characters = 1

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the end of the string, check if each suffix is a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends, and count the minimum characters needed to prepend to form a palindrome. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large strings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Characters to Add at Front to Make Palindrome
ladders.desc: Complete brute force solution to find minimum characters to prepend to make any string a palindrome
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem solution using the O(n²) brute force approach. The intuition is that to make a string palindrome by adding characters only at the front, we need to find the longest suffix that is already a palindrome. The characters we need to add are the reverse of the prefix that comes before this palindrome suffix. We start from the end of the string and check each suffix (from position i to end) to see if it's a palindrome. When we find the longest palindrome suffix starting from some position i, we know we need to add (i) characters at the front - specifically, the reverse of s[0:i]. The algorithm works by iterating i from n-1 down to 0, checking if s[i:n] is a palindrome using two-pointer comparison. Once found, we return i as the minimum characters needed. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each i we check palindrome in O(n-i) time, and worst-case when no palindrome exists until i=0, we do n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = O(n²) operations. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the palindrome checking subroutine. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, not handling empty strings, and misunderstanding that we're checking suffixes (not prefixes).

examples.trace:
String: 'abcd', n=4
Check i=3: suffix 'd' is palindrome? Compare d with d → yes. But we need longest suffix, continue checking.
Check i=2: suffix 'cd' is palindrome? Compare c with d → no.
Check i=1: suffix 'bcd' is palindrome? Compare b with d → no.
Check i=0: suffix 'abcd' is palindrome? Compare a with d → no.
Longest palindrome suffix found at i=3: 'd'
Characters needed = i = 3
Add 'dcb' to front: 'dcb' + 'abcd' = 'dcbabcd' which is palindrome.
Minimum characters = 3

String: 'aaba', n=4
Check i=3: suffix 'a' is palindrome? Compare a with a → yes.
Check i=2: suffix 'ba' is palindrome? Compare b with a → no.
Check i=1: suffix 'aba' is palindrome? Compare a with a → yes, then b with b → yes. Full palindrome!
Found palindrome suffix at i=1: 'aba'
Characters needed = i = 1
Add 'a' to end: 'aaba' + 'a' = 'aabaa' which is palindrome.
Minimum characters = 1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if a Substring is Palindrome
ladders.desc: Function to determine if a given substring is a palindrome using two-pointer technique
ladders.explanation: This is the core palindrome checking subroutine used in the main algorithm. The intuition is that a string is a palindrome if it reads the same forwards and backwards. We can check this by comparing characters from both ends moving toward the center. Given a string s and indices start and end, we initialize left=start, right=end. While left < right, we compare s[left] with s[right]. If any pair doesn't match, return False. If all pairs match, return True. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of substring (end-start+1). Space is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's called repeatedly to check each suffix. Common mistakes include using ≤ instead of < in the while condition (which would overcompare), not handling empty substrings correctly, and off-by-one errors with indices. The real pattern is simple: iterate from both ends comparing characters until pointers meet or cross.

examples.trace:
Substring: 'aba', start=0, end=2
left=0, right=2
While left<right (0<2):
  Compare s[0]='a' with s[2]='a' → match
  left=1, right=1
While left<right (1<1) → false, exit loop
All comparisons matched → return True

Substring: 'abc', start=0, end=2
left=0, right=2
While left<right (0<2):
  Compare s[0]='a' with s[2]='c' → mismatch!
  Return False immediately

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Longest Palindrome Suffix
ladders.desc: Algorithm to find the longest suffix of a string that is a palindrome
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the solution: we need to find the longest suffix that is already a palindrome. The intuition is that if we find a palindrome suffix starting at position i, then we only need to add characters corresponding to the prefix s[0:i]. We start checking from the end of the string (i=n-1) and move leftward. For each i, we check if s[i:n] is a palindrome. The first (rightmost) i where this is true gives us the longest palindrome suffix. Algorithm: initialize n = len(s). For i from n-1 down to 0: if isPalindrome(s, i, n-1) returns true, then the suffix s[i:n] is palindrome. We're done - return i. If we reach i=0 and it's not palindrome, we need to add all n characters. Time O(n²) worst case. Space O(1). This connects to L0 as it's the main loop structure. Common mistakes include checking prefixes instead of suffixes, not starting from the end, and not understanding that we want the longest (rightmost) palindrome suffix.

examples.trace:
String: 'abcd', n=4
Start i=3: check suffix 'd' (positions 3-3) → palindrome
Found at i=3 → return 3
Longest palindrome suffix is 'd' starting at position 3

String: 'aaba', n=4
Start i=3: check 'a' → palindrome, but continue for longer suffix
i=2: check 'ba' → not palindrome
i=1: check 'aba' → palindrome!
Found at i=1 → return 1
Longest palindrome suffix is 'aba' starting at position 1

ladders.title: Building Block — String Slicing and Reversal
ladders.desc: Working with substrings and reversing strings
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves extracting parts of strings and reversing them. The intuition is that to add characters at the front to make a palindrome, we need to take the prefix that's not part of the palindrome suffix and reverse it. String slicing extracts a contiguous portion: s[start:end] gives characters from start to end-1. String reversal can be done by slicing with s[::-1] or by building from the end. In our algorithm, once we find position i where suffix is palindrome, we need to add the appropriate characters to the front. For example, if s='abcd' and i=3, the non-palindrome part is s[0:3]='abc'. The correct addition is the reverse of the non-palindrome part. If palindrome starts at i, non-palindrome part is s[0:i]. Its reverse should be added. Time for slicing is O(k) where k is slice length. Space for reversal is O(k). This connects to L2 as we use this to determine what to add. Common mistakes include incorrect slice indices, misunderstanding 0-based indexing, and not accounting for the fact that we're adding at front only.

examples.trace:
String: 'abcdef'
s[0:3] means start at index 0, end at index 3 (exclusive)
Indices: 0='a', 1='b', 2='c', 3='d'
Take indices 0,1,2 → 'abc'
Result: 'abc'

String: 'hello'
Method 1: s[::-1] slice
  Start: end, stop: beginning, step: -1
  Takes: 'o','l','l','e','h' → 'olleh'
Method 2: Build from end
  Result: 'o' + 'l' + 'l' + 'e' + 'h' = 'olleh'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison in Strings
ladders.desc: Comparing individual characters at specific positions in a string
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing and comparing characters at given indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access any character by its index (0-based). To check if two positions have the same character, we use s[i] == s[j]. This is the core operation in palindrome checking. In Python-style, strings are immutable sequences, so s[i] returns the character at position i. Time O(1) for access and comparison. Space O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used repeatedly in string operations. Common mistakes include using = instead of == for comparison, index out of bounds errors, and forgetting that string indices start at 0. The pattern is straightforward: given two indices i and j, check if s[i] equals s[j].

examples.trace:
String: 'abcd'
s[0] = 'a'
s[3] = 'd'
Compare 'a' == 'd' → False
Result: False

String: 'hello'
s[2] = 'l'
s[2] = 'l' (same position)
Compare 'l' == 'l' → True
Result: True

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition and examples of palindromic strings
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. The intuition is symmetry - the first character equals the last, second equals second-last, and so on. Examples: 'racecar', 'madam', 'a', 'aa', 'aba'. Non-examples: 'hello', 'abc', 'ab'. For empty string, it's considered palindrome (reads same forward/backward). For single character, always palindrome. To check visually, write the string and its reverse - if they're identical, it's palindrome. This foundational concept connects to L4 as we need to understand what we're checking for. Common mistakes include thinking odd length is required (even length like 'abba' is also palindrome), forgetting empty/single character cases, and confusing with other patterns like anagrams.

examples.trace:
String: 'racecar'
Forward: r a c e c a r
Backward: r a c e c a r
They are identical → Palindrome

String: 'hello'
Forward: h e l l o
Backward: o l l e h
They are different → Not palindrome

approaches.name: KMP-Based O(n)
approaches.summary: Create a new string by concatenating the original string, a separator, and its reverse, then use the KMP algorithm to compute the longest prefix of the reversed string that matches a suffix of the original. The unmatched part gives the minimum characters to add. This leverages efficient pattern matching for linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Characters to Add for Palindrome Using KMP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using KMP algorithm on concatenated string to find minimum characters to add at front.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum characters to add at the front of a string to make it a palindrome. The KMP-based approach achieves O(n) time complexity by leveraging efficient pattern matching. The core idea is to create a new string S' = original + '#' + reverse(original). We then compute the KMP prefix function (also called LPS - Longest Prefix Suffix) on S'. The last value of the prefix function tells us the length of the longest prefix of the reversed string that matches a suffix of the original string. This represents the longest palindrome suffix already present in the original string. The minimum characters to add is then length(original) - this value. Intuition: If we can find how much of the end of the string already forms a palindrome with the beginning, we only need to add the characters that complete the palindrome. The separator '#' ensures we don't get matches that cross between original and reversed parts. Step-by-step: 1. Create reversed string rev = reverse(original). 2. Create concatenated string temp = original + '#' + rev. 3. Compute prefix function array pi for temp using KMP algorithm. 4. The last value pi[-1] gives the length of longest prefix of rev that matches suffix of original. 5. Answer = len(original) - pi[-1]. Time: O(n) for creating reversed string and computing prefix function. Space: O(n) for temp string and pi array. Connection to L1: This uses the KMP prefix function computation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting the separator (can cause incorrect matches), misunderstanding that we add at front only (not both ends), off-by-one errors in index calculations. Real code pattern: def min_chars_to_add(s): rev = s[::-1]; temp = s + '#' + rev; pi = [0]*len(temp); for i in range(1, len(temp)): j = pi[i-1]; while j > 0 and temp[i] != temp[j]: j = pi[j-1]; if temp[i] == temp[j]: j += 1; pi[i] = j; return len(s) - pi[-1]

examples.trace: Original: 'abcd', Reverse: 'dcba', Temp: 'abcd#dcba'. Compute prefix function: pi[0]=0 (first char always 0). i=1: compare 'b' with temp[pi[0]]='a' -> no match, pi[1]=0. i=2: 'c' vs 'a' -> no, pi[2]=0. i=3: 'd' vs 'a' -> no, pi[3]=0. i=4: '#' vs 'a' -> no, pi[4]=0. i=5: 'd' vs 'a' -> no, j=pi[4]=0, pi[5]=0. i=6: 'c' vs 'a' -> no, pi[6]=0. i=7: 'b' vs 'a' -> no, pi[7]=0. i=8: 'a' vs 'a' -> match, pi[8]=1. Last pi value = 1. Answer = 4 - 1 = 3. Need to add 'dcb' at front: 'dcbabcd'.

Original: 'aacecaaa', Reverse: 'aaacecaa', Temp: 'aacecaaa#aaacecaa'. Compute prefix function (key values): pi grows as matches occur in palindrome parts. Final pi[-1] = 7 (longest prefix of reverse matching suffix of original is 'aacecaa' length 7). Answer = 8 - 7 = 1. Need to add 'a' at front: 'aaacecaaa'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — KMP Prefix Function Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the prefix function (LPS array) for a string using KMP algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The prefix function (often called LPS - Longest Prefix which is also Suffix) is a fundamental component of the KMP string matching algorithm. For a string S of length n, the prefix function pi is an array where pi[i] is the length of the longest proper prefix of S[0..i] that is also a suffix of S[0..i]. A proper prefix means it cannot be the entire string itself. Intuition: This function helps us avoid redundant comparisons when searching for patterns by remembering how much of the pattern matches itself. The algorithm builds pi incrementally using previous values. For each position i from 1 to n-1: we maintain a pointer j = pi[i-1], then while j > 0 and S[i] != S[j], we set j = pi[j-1] (falling back to shorter prefixes). If S[i] == S[j], we increment j. Then pi[i] = j. This clever backtracking ensures O(n) time. Time: O(n) as each character is processed a constant number of times. Space: O(n) for pi array. Connection to L0: This function is used to analyze the concatenated string in the palindrome problem. Common mistakes: Starting index at 0 (pi[0] is always 0), incorrect backtracking logic, not handling empty string. Real code pattern: def compute_prefix_function(s): n = len(s); pi = [0]*n; for i in range(1, n): j = pi[i-1]; while j > 0 and s[i] != s[j]: j = pi[j-1]; if s[i] == s[j]: j += 1; pi[i] = j; return pi

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Longest Palindrome Suffix via String Concatenation
ladders.desc: The insight that concatenating original and reversed strings helps find longest palindrome suffix.
ladders.explanation: The central insight for the KMP-based palindrome approach is that by creating a new string temp = original + '#' + reverse(original) and computing its prefix function, the last value of the prefix function tells us the length of the longest prefix of the reversed string that matches a suffix of the original string. Why does this work? In a palindrome, the beginning matches the end in reverse order. If we reverse the string, the beginning of the reversed string corresponds to the end of the original. When we concatenate with a separator, the KMP prefix function on this combined string finds how much of the start (which is original) matches the end (which is reversed). Specifically, the prefix function at the end of temp gives the longest prefix of temp that is a suffix of temp. Since temp ends with the reversed string, this value represents how much of the reversed string's prefix matches a suffix of the entire temp, which due to the separator, effectively measures how much of the reversed string matches the end of the original string. This matched portion corresponds to the longest suffix of the original that is a palindrome. Step: 1. Given original s, create rev = reverse(s). 2. temp = s + '#' + rev. 3. Compute prefix function pi for temp. 4. pi[-1] gives the length of longest prefix of rev that is suffix of s. Time: O(n) for concatenation. Space: O(n) for temp. Connection to L1: This uses the prefix function computation. Common mistakes: Using wrong separator (must not appear in string), misunderstanding what pi[-1] represents. Real code pattern: s = 'abcd'; rev = s[::-1]; temp = s + '#' + rev; pi = compute_prefix_function(temp); longest_match = pi[-1]

examples.trace: s='abcd', rev='dcba', temp='abcd#dcba'. Compute pi for temp (as in L0 example): pi[-1]=1. This means longest prefix of 'dcba' matching suffix of 'abcd' is length 1 ('a' matches 'a'). So longest palindrome suffix in 'abcd' is length 1.

s='aacecaaa', rev='aaacecaa', temp='aacecaaa#aaacecaa'. Compute pi: pi[-1]=7. Longest prefix of 'aaacecaa' matching suffix of 'aacecaaa' is 'aacecaa' length 7. So longest palindrome suffix is length 7.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Reversal and Concatenation
ladders.desc: Create a new string by reversing original and concatenating with separator.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves manipulating strings to prepare for the KMP analysis. Given an input string s, we need to create a new string temp = s + '#' + reverse(s). The reversal operation creates a string that is the mirror image of the original. Concatenation combines multiple strings into one. The separator '#' is crucial—it must be a character not present in the original string to prevent accidental matches between the original and reversed parts. If '#' might appear in the string, we can use a different separator or a string that guaranteed doesn't appear. The purpose is to keep the original and reversed parts distinct so that when we compute the prefix function, we only get matches that are meaningful for finding palindrome suffixes. Intuition: By putting the reversed string after the original with a separator, we align the end of the original with the beginning of the reversed, allowing pattern matching to detect overlaps. Step-by-step: 1. Input string s. 2. Compute reversed string rev by iterating s backwards or using built-in reversal. 3. Create temp = s + '#' + rev. Time: O(n) for reversal and concatenation. Space: O(n) for new strings. Connection to L2: This creates the string needed for the core logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting separator, using separator that appears in string, inefficient reversal. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; rev = ''; for i in range(len(s)-1, -1, -1): rev += s[i]; temp = s + '#' + rev

examples.trace: Original: 'abcd'. Reverse: start from end: 'd','c','b','a' -> 'dcba'. Concatenate: 'abcd' + '#' + 'dcba' = 'abcd#dcba'.
Original: 'aba'. Reverse: 'a','b','a' -> 'aba'. Concatenate: 'aba' + '#' + 'aba' = 'aba#aba'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Reversal
ladders.desc: Create a new string that is the reverse of the original string.
ladders.explanation: String reversal is a fundamental operation where we create a new string with characters in opposite order. For a string s of length n, the reversed string rev has rev[i] = s[n-1-i] for all i from 0 to n-1. Intuition: Reversal mirrors the string, which is essential for palindrome problems since palindromes read the same forwards and backwards. The algorithm can be implemented by iterating from the end to the beginning or using two-pointer approach. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty string rev. 2. For i from n-1 down to 0: append s[i] to rev. Alternatively, use built-in functions like s[::-1] in Python. Time: O(n) as we visit each character once. Space: O(n) for the new string. Connection to L3: This is used in creating the concatenated string. Common mistakes: In-place reversal for immutable strings (not possible in many languages), off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; rev = ''; for i in range(len(s)-1, -1, -1): rev += s[i]

examples.trace: s='abcd', length=4. i=3: s[3]='d' -> rev='d'. i=2: s[2]='c' -> rev='dc'. i=1: s[1]='b' -> rev='dcb'. i=0: s[0]='a' -> rev='dcba'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding palindromes: strings that read same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. Examples: 'racecar', 'madam', 'abba', 'a'. For a string s of length n, it is a palindrome if s[i] == s[n-1-i] for all i from 0 to n-1. Intuition: Palindromes have symmetry around the center. This concept is foundational for the problem because we want to make a string into a palindrome by adding characters. Key properties: 1. Single-character strings are always palindromes. 2. Empty string is considered a palindrome. 3. Even-length palindromes have pairs of matching characters (e.g., 'abba'). 4. Odd-length palindromes have a center character that doesn't need a pair (e.g., 'aba'). To check if a string is a palindrome, we can compare characters from both ends moving inward. Step-by-step: 1. Set left=0, right=len(s)-1. 2. While left < right: if s[left] != s[right], return false; else left++, right--. 3. Return true. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Reversal helps in palindrome checking since reversed string should equal original. Common mistakes: Not handling empty string, off-by-one in indices, case sensitivity (if applicable). Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): left=0; right=len(s)-1; while left<right: if s[left]!=s[right]: return False; left+=1; right-=1; return True

examples.trace: s='aba', length=3. left=0 ('a'), right=2 ('a'): match. left=1, right=1: loop ends (left not < right). Return true.
s='abc', length=3. left=0 ('a'), right=2 ('c'): no match. Return false immediately.

approaches.name: Two Pointer with Greedy Expansion O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers starting from both ends of the string, greedily expand inward while checking for palindrome conditions, and track mismatches to determine the minimum additions needed. This approach is efficient and intuitive, avoiding full string reversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Characters to Add at Front to Make Palindrome
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two-pointer greedy expansion to find minimum characters to add at front to make any string a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum number of characters to add ONLY at the FRONT of a given string to make it a palindrome. The two-pointer greedy expansion approach provides an O(n) solution by efficiently identifying the longest palindromic suffix. The intuition: we want to find the longest suffix of the string that is already a palindrome, then we only need to add the reverse of the remaining prefix to the front. We use two pointers starting from both ends, moving inward while characters match. When they don't match, we reset the right pointer to the end and continue from the next left position, effectively checking different suffix lengths. This greedy approach ensures we find the longest palindromic suffix without checking all possibilities. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left=0, right=len(s)-1, last_match=right. 2. While left < right: if s[left]==s[right], move both inward (left++, right--). Else: reset right to last_match, left moves to next starting position (left = left - (last_match - right) + 1), reset last_match. 3. When left >= right, we've found the longest palindromic suffix starting from some position. The minimum additions = left (the index where the non-palindromic prefix ends). Time: O(n) - each character visited at most twice. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the palindrome checking subroutine from L1 but with reset logic to find the longest suffix. Common mistakes: Not handling reset correctly when mismatch occurs, off-by-one errors in calculating additions, misunderstanding that we add at front only. Real pattern: Use while loop with two pointers and reset logic when mismatch found.

examples.trace: Step 1: s='abcd', left=0, right=3, last_match=3. s[0]='a', s[3]='d' mismatch. Reset: right=3, left=1, last_match=3. Step 2: left=1, right=3. s[1]='b', s[3]='d' mismatch. Reset: right=3, left=2, last_match=3. Step 3: left=2, right=3. s[2]='c', s[3]='d' mismatch. Reset: right=3, left=3, last_match=3. Step 4: left=3, right=3 (left>=right). Minimum additions = left = 3. Add 'dcb' to front: 'dcbabcd' is palindrome.

s='aacecaaa', n=8. Initialize i=0, j=7. i<j (0<7): s[0]='a', s[7]='a' → match, i=1, j=6. i<j (1<6): s[1]='a', s[6]='a' → match, i=2, j=5. i<j (2<5): s[2]='c', s[5]='a' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=4. i<j (0<4): s[0]='a', s[4]='c' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=3. i<j (0<3): s[0]='a', s[3]='e' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=2. i<j (0<2): s[0]='a', s[2]='c' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=1. i<j (0<1): s[0]='a', s[1]='a' → match, i=1, j=0. i>=j (1>=0): exit. Minimum additions = i = 1. Add 'a' to front: 'a' + 'aacecaaa' = 'aaacecaaa' which is palindrome.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking if a String is Palindrome Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Helper function that checks if a given string is a palindrome using two pointers moving inward.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core building block for the main problem. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. The two-pointer approach provides an efficient O(n) check by comparing characters from both ends moving toward the center. Intuition: If a string is a palindrome, then for every i, s[i] should equal s[n-1-i]. We can verify this by starting with pointers at both ends and moving inward until they meet. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left=0, right=len(s)-1. 2. While left < right: if s[left] != s[right], return False. Else, left++, right--. 3. If loop completes without mismatch, return True. Time: O(n) where n is string length. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This check is used within the main algorithm to verify potential suffixes. In the full problem, we don't explicitly call this function for every suffix (which would be O(n²)), but the greedy expansion embeds this logic. Common mistakes: Using for loop instead of while, incorrect termination condition (left <= right vs left < right), not handling empty string. Real pattern: Simple while loop with two pointers comparing characters.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=6. s[0]='r', s[6]='r' match. left=1, right=5. Step 2: s[1]='a', s[5]='a' match. left=2, right=4. Step 3: s[2]='c', s[4]='c' match. left=3, right=3. left>=right, exit loop. Return True.
Step 1: left=0, right=4. s[0]='h', s[4]='o' mismatch. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Longest Palindromic Suffix with Greedy Expansion
ladders.desc: The central technique of expanding inward from ends while handling mismatches to find the longest suffix that is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the efficient O(n) solution. Instead of checking every possible suffix (which would be O(n²)), we use a greedy approach that finds the longest palindromic suffix in linear time. Intuition: We want to find the largest index i such that s[i...n-1] is a palindrome. We start checking from the full string (i=0) and use two pointers. When we find a mismatch, we know the current suffix isn't a palindrome, so we move the starting point i forward and try again. The key insight: we don't need to restart checking from the beginning each time - we can greedily continue from where we left off. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize i=0, j=n-1. 2. While i < j: if s[i]==s[j], i++, j--. Else: i=0, j-- (reset to check a shorter suffix). 3. When i >= j, we've found the longest palindromic suffix starting from some position. The minimum additions needed = i. Time: O(n) because j decreases at most n times and i resets but total operations are linear. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the palindrome checking logic but with reset mechanism. Connection to L0: This directly gives the solution - the number of additions is i. Common mistakes: Not resetting i to 0 when mismatch occurs, incorrect calculation of additions. Real pattern: While loop with two pointers and reset when mismatch found.

examples.trace: s='abcd', n=4. Initialize i=0, j=3. i<j (0<3): s[0]='a', s[3]='d' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=2. i<j (0<2): s[0]='a', s[2]='c' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=1. i<j (0<1): s[0]='a', s[1]='b' → mismatch. Reset i=0, j=0. i>=j (0>=0): exit. Minimum additions = 3.

Step 1: i=0, j=7. s[0]='a', s[7]='a' match. i=1, j=6. Step 2: s[1]='a', s[6]='a' match. i=2, j=5. Step 3: s[2]='c', s[5]='a' mismatch. Since i!=0, reset i=0. Now i=0, j=5. Step 4: s[0]='a', s[5]='a' match. i=1, j=4. Step 5: s[1]='a', s[4]='c' mismatch. i!=0, reset i=0. i=0, j=4. Step 6: s[0]='a', s[4]='c' mismatch. i==0, so j=3. i=0, j=3. Step 7: s[0]='a', s[3]='e' mismatch. i==0, j=2. i=0, j=2. Step 8: s[0]='a', s[2]='c' mismatch. i==0, j=1. i=0, j=1. Step 9: s[0]='a', s[1]='a' match. i=1, j=0. Exit. i=1. Minimum additions = 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding String Suffixes and Palindromes
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of identifying all suffixes of a string and checking which are palindromes.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for understanding the problem. A suffix is a contiguous sequence that ends at the string's end. For string of length n, there are n suffixes: s[0...n-1], s[1...n-1], ..., s[n-1...n-1]. The problem reduces to finding the longest suffix that is a palindrome, because then we only need to add the reverse of the remaining prefix to the front. Intuition: If we find a suffix of length k that is a palindrome, then the prefix of length n-k needs to be reversed and added to the front. The minimum additions correspond to the maximum k where suffix is palindrome. Step-by-step to find all palindromic suffixes: 1. For i from 0 to n-1: 2. Check if s[i...n-1] is palindrome using two-pointer check. 3. Track the largest i where palindrome is true. Time: O(n²) if done naively (n suffixes each checked in O(n)). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: The greedy algorithm optimizes this to O(n) by avoiding explicit checks for every suffix. Connection to L1: Uses palindrome checking subroutine. Common mistakes: Confusing suffix with substring, off-by-one in indices, not considering empty suffix. Real pattern: Loop through starting indices and check palindrome from that index to end.

examples.trace: Suffixes: 'abcd' (i=0) - not palindrome (a!=d). 'bcd' (i=1) - not palindrome (b!=d). 'cd' (i=2) - not palindrome (c!=d). 'd' (i=3) - palindrome (single character). Longest is 'd' at i=3. Prefix length = 3, so need 3 additions.

Check palindromic prefixes of 'aacecaaa' from longest to shortest: 'aacecaaa' (length 8): pos0=a, pos7=a ✓; pos1=a, pos6=a ✓; pos2=c, pos5=a ✗ - not palindrome. 'aacecaa' (length 7): pos0=a, pos6=a ✓; pos1=a, pos5=a ✓; pos2=c, pos4=c ✓; pos3=e center ✓ - palindrome! Longest palindromic prefix is 'aacecaa' (length 7). Additions needed = 8 - 7 = 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Characters and Moving Pointers
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing two characters and incrementing/decrementing indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic operation used throughout the algorithm. It involves accessing characters at specific indices, comparing them for equality, and updating pointer positions. Intuition: String algorithms often involve traversing the string from different directions, and pointer manipulation is key to efficient traversal. Step-by-step: 1. Access s[i] and s[j] using array indexing. 2. Compare using equality operator (==). 3. If equal, increment i (i++) and decrement j (j--). 4. If not equal, handle mismatch according to algorithm logic. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly when checking if a suffix is palindrome. Connection to L2: The greedy algorithm uses this with specific reset rules. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds, confusing i++ with ++i, not updating both pointers when needed. Real pattern: Simple if-else with pointer updates.

examples.output: mismatch, i=0, j=1 (if following reset rules)
examples.trace: Step 1: Access s[0]='a', s[2]='c'. Compare: 'a' == 'c'? False. Mismatch. According to basic operation, we don't update i and j automatically - the algorithm decides. For demonstration: if algorithm says on mismatch: j--, then new j=1. So i=0, j=1.
Step 1: Access s[0]='a', s[2]='a'. Compare: 'a' == 'a'? True. Match. Update: i=0+1=1, j=2-1=1. Now i=1, j=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: understanding palindrome definition and examples.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. For strings, this means the first character equals the last, second equals second-last, and so on. Intuition: Palindromes have symmetry around their center. Examples: 'racecar', 'madam', 'aa', 'a'. Empty string and single character are trivially palindromes. Key properties: 1. If a string is palindrome, reversing it gives the same string. 2. Any substring of a palindrome is not necessarily palindrome. 3. Palindromes can be even or odd length. Step-by-step to check manually: Write the string and its reverse, compare character by character. Time to check: O(n) by comparing pairs. Space: O(n) if storing reverse, O(1) if using two pointers. Connection to L4: This concept leads to the character comparison operation. Connection to L3: We need this to understand palindromic suffixes. Common mistakes: Thinking 'ab' is palindrome, ignoring case sensitivity, not considering empty string. Real pattern: Understanding symmetry in sequences.

examples.trace: Forward: r-a-c-e-c-a-r. Backward: r-a-c-e-c-a-r. They are identical. Check pairs: position 0 and 6: r==r. 1 and 5: a==a. 2 and 4: c==c. 3 and 3: e==e (center). All match.
Forward: h-e-l-l-o. Backward: o-l-l-e-h. Compare: position 0: h vs o mismatch. So not palindrome.
